summary: Fowler addresses criticism that his book lacks enterprise architecture content by distinguishing between enterprise application architecture (designing individual applications) and enterprise architecture (organizing multiple applications). He explains why enterprise architecture initiatives typically fail: they're driven by IT management but must work with applications controlled by business sponsors who prioritize their own delivery timelines. The political reality is that application teams with business backing will always win against central architecture groups. Success requires submitting to these political forces by focusing on incremental business value, minimizing application team costs, and favoring loosely-coupled integration approaches like messaging.
key_points: Enterprise architecture initiatives follow a predictable boom-bust cycle: grand launch, declining influence, quiet death, then repeat
Central architecture groups fail because they lack the political power of application teams backed by business sponsors
The business sponsor holds the 'ace card' - they'll back application teams who resist architecture mandates that delay delivery
Success requires focusing on integration techniques rather than overarching plans, with minimal impact on application teams
Good application architecture (especially presentation-domain separation) makes enterprise integration much easier
money_quotes: Two or three years later, not much has been done and the enterprise architecture group isn't getting their phone calls returned.
The problem for central architecture groups is that they are driven by IT management, but the applications they are looking to organize are driven by business needs.
Since the application is directly connected to providing business value, and the central architectural team isn't, the application team wins.
These initiatives should be less about building an overarching plan for applications, and more about coming up with techniques to integrate applications in whatever way they are put together.
Application boundaries are primarily social constructs and they aren't likely to conform to anyone's forward plans.
I think you also need to focus on approaches that minimize coupling between applications, even if such approaches are less efficient than a more tightly coupled approach might be.
key_insight: Enterprise architecture initiatives fail not due to technical shortcomings but because they lack the political power of application teams who have business sponsors backing their priorities.
